name: Signup Flow
summary: Patterns for high-converting signup experiences that minimize friction and maximize activation.

patterns.name: Progressive disclosure
patterns.description: Don't show all fields at once. Break signup into logical steps, revealing fields progressively as the user commits to the process.
do: Split into 2-3 logical steps (identity → details → preferences)
Allow going back to previous steps
Save progress so users can return later
dont: Show all 15 fields on one page
Ask for optional fields during initial signup
Require credit card on free trial signup
Ask for information you can auto-detect (timezone, country)
patterns.evidence: Reducing form fields from 11 to 4 increased conversions by 120% (Imagescape). HubSpot saw a 5% conversion increase per field removed.

patterns.name: Social login as primary
patterns.description: Offer social/SSO login as the primary path, with email signup as secondary. Reduces friction to near-zero for users with Google/Apple/GitHub accounts.
do: Place social login buttons prominently above email form
Support Google, Apple, and GitHub for developer tools
Auto-fill profile data from social account
dont: Offer more than 4 social login options (paradox of choice)
Require additional profile completion after social signup
Use social login buttons that look like regular buttons
patterns.evidence: Social login can increase signup rates by 20-40%. Google Sign-In alone accounts for over 50% of social logins on most platforms.

patterns.name: Single-field step-through
patterns.description: Show one field at a time with large input areas and auto-advance. Creates a focused, low-cognitive-load experience. Works especially well on mobile.
do: Auto-focus the input field on each step
Auto-advance after valid selection (no submit button needed)
dont: Use for more than 5-6 steps — it becomes tedious
Remove the ability to see/edit previous answers
Auto-advance on text fields — only on selection fields
patterns.evidence: Typeform-style single-field forms see 3-4x higher completion rates than traditional multi-field forms for surveys and lead capture.

patterns.name: Value-first onboarding
patterns.description: Let users experience value before requiring signup. Show the product working, then gate further usage behind account creation.
do: Let users try the core feature without signing up
Gate saves, exports, or collaboration behind signup
Show what they'll get by signing up in context
Preserve any work done before signup
dont: Require signup before showing any product value
Lose the user's pre-signup work after account creation
Show a generic landing page instead of the product
patterns.evidence: Products that allow value-first usage see 2-3x higher signup rates. Canva, Figma, and Notion all use this pattern.

checklist: Is the value proposition clear before the user starts signing up?
Can a user complete signup in under 60 seconds?
Are only essential fields required at signup?
Is there a social/SSO login option?
Does the form use appropriate input types and autocomplete?
Is there a progress indicator for multi-step flows?
Can the user go back and edit previous steps?
Is inline validation provided (not just on submit)?
Does the CTA button have clear, action-oriented text ('Create account', not 'Submit')?
Is there a clear path if the user already has an account?
Does the form work well on mobile?
Is progress saved if the user leaves and returns?
